title: Unable to pull signed images using manifest/envelope
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

/kind bug


**Description**

Unable to pull signed images using manifest.

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

On one client, I am pulling down ubuntu 20.04 images for amd64 and arm architectures. I then push (and sign) those images to a local (private) registry. I then create a manifest from those images and push/sign to the same local (private) registry.

On the other client, podman is used to pull down an image based off the manifest (note: public key of the signer has been copied locally and configured in /etc/containers/registry.d/<registry.local>). When I pull by the manifest, I get an error stating that 'signature for identity .... not accepted'. Pulling down the amd64 image directly works fine.

Aside from the actual registry host name and pgp name, the commands are verbatim:

Host A:
```
podman pull ubuntu@sha256:1d7b639619bdca2d008eca2d5293e3c43ff84cbee597ff76de3b7a7de3e84956
podman pull ubuntu@sha256:ebc8925ee51929a9d691f260b34dd6ed59b4cc2f6ca61e515ce08d31d38e0a87

podman tag ubuntu@sha256:1d7b639619bdca2d008eca2d5293e3c43ff84cbee597ff76de3b7a7de3e84956 myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-amd64
podman tag ubuntu@sha256:ebc8925ee51929a9d691f260b34dd6ed59b4cc2f6ca61e515ce08d31d38e0a87 myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-arm

podman push --sign-by myteam myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-amd64
podman push --sign-by myteam myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-arm

podman manifest create ubuntu:20.04
podman manifest add ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-amd64
podman manifest add ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-arm
podman manifest push --sign-by myteam --format v2s2 ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04
```

Take note: the signatures created, above, are accessible via http (myregistry.local:8000) as the output shows below.

Host B:

pulling by manifest:
```
podman pull --log-level debug myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04

DEBU[0000] Using conmon: "/usr/libexec/podman/conmon"

From the output in each, I can see the signature files both commands are pulling are for the same image (sha's match).

**Describe the results you received:**

I was expecting pull by manifest to succeed as if pulling directly using the arch specific tag

**Describe the results you expected:**

Pulling by the manifest failed, whereas pulling directly by the arch specific tag worked (passed validation)


**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Podman version 2.1.1

[Docker Registry](https://hub.docker.com/layers/registry/library/registry/2.7.1/images/sha256-e09ed8c6c837d366a501f15dcb47939bbbb6242bf3886270834e2a0fa1555234?context=explore) version 2.7.1

Clients: Ubuntu 20.04

comments.body: @QiWang19 PTAL

@causalityloop I can reproduce the error. Looks like the error was caused by the sign not applied to each OS/ARCH of the manifest list. You can add `--all` to the command you used to push the manifest list. `podman manifest push --all --sign-by myteam --format v2s2 ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04`. And before push it to docker hub, I also run `podman tag ubuntu myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04`.
thank you for the reply. give me a day to circle back and try this out

I tried the --all flag and had some mixed results.

In the below scenario, Client A uploads the images and Client B performs the signs.
----------

Client A pull/tag/push - no sign by this client

```
podman pull ubuntu@sha256:1d7b639619bdca2d008eca2d5293e3c43ff84cbee597ff76de3b7a7de3e84956
podman pull ubuntu@sha256:ebc8925ee51929a9d691f260b34dd6ed59b4cc2f6ca61e515ce08d31d38e0a87
podman tag ubuntu@sha256:1d7b639619bdca2d008eca2d5293e3c43ff84cbee597ff76de3b7a7de3e84956 myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-amd64
podman tag ubuntu@sha256:ebc8925ee51929a9d691f260b34dd6ed59b4cc2f6ca61e515ce08d31d38e0a87 myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-arm
podman push myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-amd64
podman push myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-arm
```

Client B (perform actual sign)
```
podman image sign --sign-by myteam --directory /sigstore docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-amd64
podman image sign --sign-by myteam --directory /sigstore docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-arm

```
/sigstore contents

```
drwxr-x--x 2 root root 4096 Nov  9 12:32 'ubuntu@sha256=9648e4271c723c5df3b207bbaac8bee6ef48ca3a55dc0e10f2f9106aad11fe3c'
drwxr-x--x 2 root root 4096 Nov  9 12:32 'ubuntu@sha256=c58b969e6e560af8a68a12c158f390cbcc13b3b4a391e74e9e208bf093597185'
```

Client A pulls back down signed (successful) (note: signatures are provided over https where /sigstore is hosted/accessible via nginx)

```
podman pull myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-amd64

Trying to pull myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-amd64...

Client A create manifest (no sign)

```
podman manifest create ubuntu:20.04
podman manifest add ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-amd64
podman manifest add ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-arm
podman manifest push --format v2s2 ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04
```

Client B create manifest (perform actual sign)

```
podman manifest create ubuntu:20.04
podman manifest add ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-amd64
podman manifest add ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04-arm
podman manifest push --all --sign-by myteam --format v2s2 ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04
Getting image list signatures
Copying 2 of 2 images in list

@causalityloop: The label(s) `sig/contents` cannot be applied, because the repository doesn't have them

(I haven’t tried to reproduce nor have I re-checked what exactly the various Podman commands do, this is just a very quick note hoping that it might be useful:)

The last section of https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/8223#issuecomment-724292204 works basically as I’d expect.

(To be explicit, note that the signature code is completely unaware of tagging conventions, if any: `20.04-arm` is not considered to have any relationship to `20.04`, just like `20.04-i-like-january` has no relationship to `20.04`. It enforces strict equality, or more precisely see `matchRepoDigestOrExact` in https://github.com/containers/image/blob/master/docs/containers-policy.json.5.md .)

The way signature checking works, the multi-arch manifest list does not have to be signed (although I vaguely remember some code does create a signature), but the individual per-arch images _do_ have to be signed – using the identity _used to pull the image_. So, for a `podman pull …:20.04`, the individual per-arch images must be signed using a `:20.04` identity. Signing with a `20.04-arm` is neither sufficient nor necessary.

If you want to allow _both_ pulling a `…:20.04` multi-arch image, and each individual image using a per-arch `…:20.04-arm` tag, each per-arch image must have two signatures, one with `…:20.04` and one with `…:20.04-arm`.

Something to note: when signing an arch specific image multiple times, it will create files called signature-1, signature-2, signature-n and so on. On the pull, podman will iterate and grab all signature-* files and begin to verify when it locates them all. Would the fix here be that in creating the manifest, we follow the same suit as signing an arch image whereas if signature-1 exists we will then create signature-2? It appears the outcome of creating a signed manifest is that signature-1 is created and if it already exists - it is overwritten. To add, if we had signed the amd64 image multiple times, such that we now have signature-1, signature-2, and signature-3 - now creating the manifest, it will overwrite signature-1 **and** delete everything else in that folder (signature-2 and signature-3)

So rereading more carefully…

> @causalityloop I can reproduce the error. Looks like the error was caused by the sign not applied to each OS/ARCH of the manifest list. You can add `--all` to the command you used to push the manifest list. `podman manifest push --all --sign-by myteam --format v2s2 ubuntu:20.04 docker://myregistry.local/ubuntu:20.04`.

This looks like a fairly clear bug: it’s very reasonable to expect that `podman manifest push --sign-by $tag` will create a valid signature, but that uses `pushOptions.ImageListSelection = cp.CopySpecificImages` with empty `pushOptions.Instances`, which causes `copy.Image` to sign the manifest list (not used for anything), but not the individual images. `podman manifest push --sign-by `**`--all`**` $tag` triggers the signing of all components.

It is ambiguous whether `copy.Image` with `Options{SignBy: …, ImageListSelection: CopySpecificImages}` is expected to sign the images it does not copy (the documentation of `SignBy` says “for a signature to be added during the copy”, and is there a copy of the non-listed images?). More importantly, the `c/image/types.ImageDestination` API currently explicitly documents that signatures can only be added after `PutManifest` is called (i.e. basically we have to make a full copy), and `ImageDestination` is an user-facing interface we can’t trivially break, so it isn’t quite an option for `copy.Image` in this situation to automatically add signatures to all components.

I’m leaning towards rejecting `Options{SignBy: …, ImageListSelection: CopySpecificImages}` (at least if any per-arch instances are excluded). What do others think? Cc: @nalind .

Independently, `podman manifest push` should probably warn/refuse `--sign-by` without `--all`, or maybe automatically use `--all` with `--sign-by` (does that make sense at all? I haven’t read the Podman manifest code) to get a better error message than something referring to `copy.Options{SignBy, ImageListSelection}`, terms unknown to the user.

> Something to note: when signing an arch specific image multiple times, it will create files called signature-1, signature-2, signature-n and so on. On the pull, podman will iterate and grab all signature-* files and begin to verify when it locates them all. Would the fix here be that in creating the manifest, we follow the same suit as signing an arch image whereas if signature-1 exists we will then create signature-2?

Yes: with your naming scheme, there needs to be a `signature-1` and `signature-2`, one signing `…:20.04` and one signing
`…:20.04-arm`.

> It appears the outcome of creating a signed manifest is that signature-1 is created and if it already exists - it is overwritten.

That seems like something that could happen if `manifest list add` is a metadata-only operation that does not pull the image.

But with `podman manifest push --all` doing a full copy, it should _also_ copy the signatures from the source. Is the computer doing the second signing operation configured to read the relevant `sigstore` so that it can see the first signature? E.g. with an enforcing policy, can you pull the first signed tag?

I’d expect the debug log to contain lines like
```
DEBU[0001] GET http://myregistry.local:8000/ubuntu@sha256=c58b969e6e560af8a68a12c158f390cbcc13b3b4a391e74e9e208bf093597185/signature-1
```
(or similar lines for reading local files, if the sigstore is local) for signature-{1,2,…}, up to the total number of pre-existing signatures *plus 1*.

(Note: I haven’t checked what containers/common/pkg/supplemented does with signatures.)

@mtrmac @zgrossbart where do we stand with this?

Per https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/8223#issuecomment-864090528 , `podman manifest push --sign-by` should not use `pushOptions.ImageListSelection = cp.CopySpecificImages` with empty `pushOptions.Instances`; then c/image can start rejecting that.

---

There may be other problems that also need fixing.
